network.overall_strategy: The network centers on literature-supported prebiotic routes to threonine that converge on four experimentally or conceptually justified hubs: HCN, acetaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile. The dominant route follows cyanosulfidic surface photochemistry in which HCN is photoreduced to aldehydes, acetaldehyde is converted through its cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde, and lactaldehyde or glyceraldehyde undergo Strecker chemistry to the threonine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine. Parallel target-producing routes include spark-discharge reservoir chemistry, Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde, low-selectivity HCN polymer hydrolysate chemistry, and a speculative but literature-motivated reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate under mild hydrothermal/prebiotic conditions. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively and literature-aligned: vents generate and concentrate reduced carbon species such as formate and pyruvate on FeS/Fe3S4/magnetite systems, feeding surface aldehyde and amino acid networks and biochemical assembly. The network explicitly treats threonine as a fragile terminal product and routes some pathways through immediate downstream use in peptide formation.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent-like, pH 9-11, H2-rich, thermophoretic concentration in micropores

reactions.mechanism: Fe-S mineral-mediated reduction of CO2 by hydrothermal reducing power generates formate as an entry point to reduced carbon chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formate generation on FeS/Fe3S4 in alkaline vent analogs is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent geochemistry; mackinawite and greigite precipitate in Hadean chimneys at 40-80C and support reduced carbon formation and concentration (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012; Shock & Canovas, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation on iron-sulfur minerals

conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal or vent-mixing regime; reduced, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Carbonylated iron-sulfur chemistry fixes reduced carbon into an alpha-keto acid pool, represented here by pyruvate, which can feed later hydroxy-keto acid chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation under hydrothermal iron-sulfur conditions is supported by carbonylated FeS chemistry; this step is included as a conservative hydrothermal source of alpha-keto acid carbon rather than a direct threonine route (Cody et al., 2000).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox complexes with mineral-assisted UV photochemistry
conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; H2S as reductant; phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic setting

reactions.mechanism: UV photoredox cycling of cyanide chemistry reduces and homologates carbon, generating low-molecular-weight aldehydes including glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally generated formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN via Cu(I) cyanide/H2S UV photoredox chemistry, and Patel et al. embedded this in a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism. TiO2 is included as a plausible surface photocatalytic mineral support for UV chemistry in shallow pools (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Dunn et al., 1981).

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer homologation from glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry with borate stabilization
conditions.other: UV-exposed surface pool; cyanide homologation; borate-stabilized sugar chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Reductive cyanide homologation extends glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde under cyanosulfidic photochemistry, with borate stabilizing the higher sugar aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. showed that cyanide homologation chemistry produces sugar precursors central to ribonucleotide synthesis, and glyceraldehyde is specifically identified in the report as a direct entry point to threonine aminonitrile. Borate minerals are included because they stabilize sugars and related aldehydes in surface environments (Patel et al., 2015; de Marcellus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: 2 h UV irradiation; H2S reductant; reported 19% lactaldehyde yield from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, then UV photoreduction converts that intermediate to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key experimentally demonstrated threonine-specific precursor step. Ritson and Sutherland reported photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde in 19% yield after 2 h irradiation under cyanosulfidic conditions. TiO2 is added as a plausible mineral photocatalytic support in UV-exposed pools without changing the core literature route (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral surface concentration and cyanide activation
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions in evaporitic or photochemical pool; racemic, diastereomeric aminonitrile expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, followed by cyanide addition to generate 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies this as the best-characterized route to the threonine aminonitrile and notes classic Strecker conversion of lactaldehyde with NH3 and HCN. Montmorillonite is used here as a plausible concentrating surface in pools, while sulfide minerals are compatible with cyanosulfidic settings (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: surface pool chemistry; direct amino acid precursor branch from RNA precursor aldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine and then with cyanide to produce threonine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified glyceraldehyde as a parallel route to the threonine aminonitrile and as a branch point connecting RNA and amino acid precursor chemistry. Borate is included because it stabilizes sugar aldehydes in surface settings (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis in pools, brines, or interfaces; threonine/allothreonine racemic mixture expected

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide-like intermediates to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine is the terminal step implied in Strecker chemistry and explicitly described in the deep report. Mild conditions are favored because threonine is thermally and hydrolytically fragile (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: ammonium carbonate present; hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide and ammonium carbonate to form a hydantoin intermediate that sequesters the amino acid precursor in a more stable cyclic form.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies the Bucherer-Bergs route as a thermodynamically favorable alternative to direct Strecker chemistry and specifically proposes 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the threonine precursor from lactaldehyde. Mineral concentration in surface pools is added to satisfy environmental realism (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases the amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the defining second stage of the Bucherer-Bergs pathway and provides a route to threonine from the lactaldehyde-derived hydantoin. This path is grounded in the demonstrated prebiotic amino-acid hydantoin chemistry of Pulletikurti et al. and the meteoritic occurrence of hydantoins (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge reservoir synthesis of HCN, ammonia, acetaldehyde, and glycine

conditions.temperature: electrical discharge with hot-water mist; bulk collection near ambient
conditions.catalyst: spark discharge energy with condensation into aqueous reservoir
conditions.other: CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S/H2O-type discharge atmospheres; racemic abiotic products; aldehydes and amino acids collected in aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates reactive radicals and nitriles in the gas phase, which collapse into an aqueous reservoir containing HCN, NH3, aldehydes, and amino acids including glycine and threonine precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. first confirmed threonine/allothreonine in discharge products, and Parker et al. showed H2S-containing Miller samples produce a broader amino acid suite including threonine. This reaction is represented explicitly as a precursor-reservoir step rather than incorrectly mapping spark chemistry onto cyanosulfidic photochemistry (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; low-yield and thermodynamically unfavorable; forms threonine and allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Reverse of threonine aldol cleavage: C-C bond formation between glycine-derived nucleophile and acetaldehyde gives beta-hydroxy-alpha-amino acid products.
reactions.reasoning: This route is included only as a weak, explicitly speculative connection because the literature identifies the reverse aldol relationship but notes the forward condensation is thermodynamically unfavorable and lacks a clean prebiotic demonstration. It is retained to reflect a literature-discussed structural route, not a strongly validated pathway (Fesko, 2016).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN and polymeric nitrile reservoir

conditions.other: concentrated cyanide solutions or drying surface films

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes oligomerization to diaminomaleonitrile and related nitrile polymers, creating a broad reactive reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: The report notes HCN oligomer chemistry as a broad-spectrum source of organics and identifies DAMN as a major oligomeric intermediate. This is a low-selectivity feeder branch rather than a targeted threonine route (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer reservoir to amino acid mixture including threonine

conditions.other: complex mixture hydrolysis; low selectivity; threonine expected only as minor component

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis and rearrangement of nitrile-rich HCN polymers release mixtures of amino acids, among which threonine can appear at low abundance.
reactions.reasoning: The report states that threonine has been detected in hydrolysates of HCN polymers under a range of conditions, but as part of complex low-selectivity amino acid mixtures. This step is therefore intentionally represented as diffuse and weakly selective (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from pyruvate-derived alpha-keto acid chemistry

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+/Fe-S mineral promoted alpha-keto acid network
conditions.other: mild vent or vent-mixing conditions; included as a conservative analog extension of hydrothermal keto-acid chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate-centered alpha-keto acid chemistry is extended to a beta-hydroxylated C4 keto acid pool under reducing mineral-catalyzed hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This is the most speculative step in the network and is labeled accordingly. The specific prebiotic formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate was not directly demonstrated in the cited work, but alpha-keto acid networks and hydrothermal pyruvate formation are supported, and threonine's keto-acid precursor is identified in the report. This step is included only because the research explicitly highlights reductive amination of the corresponding keto acid as a plausible route while leaving precursor-generation unresolved (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+ in water at 70C or Ni/H2 at 22C, pH 8
conditions.other: conceptual extension of demonstrated keto-acid reductive amination to the threonine-specific substrate

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by reduction to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly identifies reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as a plausible route to threonine by analogy to experimentally demonstrated conversion of other alpha-keto acids to amino acids under Fe2+ or Ni/H2 conditions. This exact substrate has not been directly tested, so the route is retained but marked as literature-motivated rather than directly demonstrated (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-mediated peptide formation from threonine

conditions.other: repeated hydration-dehydration cycles in hot spring or evaporitic setting; immediate utilization mitigates threonine instability

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation links threonine monomers into short peptides during repeated drying and rewetting.
reactions.reasoning: This terminal use step addresses the literature-emphasized fragility of threonine by routing it rapidly into macromolecular assembly. Wet-dry cycling and mineral interfaces are well supported for peptide formation, though not threonine-specific (Lahav et al., 1978; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1998; Damer & Deamer, 2020; Campbell et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Best-supported route from HCN through acetaldehyde and lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Parallel RNA-linked route where glycolaldehyde is homologated to glyceraldehyde, which directly gives threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to lactaldehyde branch
pathways.description: Electrical discharge generates HCN, ammonia, and acetaldehyde reservoirs that feed the validated lactaldehyde-Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to glyceraldehyde branch
pathways.description: Discharge-generated HCN feeds surface cyanosulfidic homologation into the glyceraldehyde route to threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde is trapped as a hydantoin intermediate, then hydrolyzed to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate route
pathways.description: Low-selectivity amino acid formation from HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis, included as a background abiotic source of threonine.

pathways.name: Aldol condensation route from spark-generated glycine and acetaldehyde
pathways.description: Speculative but literature-discussed reverse-aldol relationship linking glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal alpha-keto acid chemistry leads to the threonine keto-acid precursor, followed by reductive amination under mild prebiotic conditions.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface hybrid lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon and concentrated reactants support transfer into surface cyanosulfidic aldehyde chemistry and the validated lactaldehyde-Strecker branch.

pathways.name: Rapid utilization route via peptide capture
pathways.description: Threonine formed through the main aminonitrile route is immediately incorporated into short peptides to mitigate instability.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest and most literature-aligned route. The acetaldehyde-to-lactaldehyde photoreduction and subsequent Strecker conversion to threonine aminonitrile are well supported by the Ritson-Sutherland and Patel cyanosulfidic framework. Minor uncertainty remains in the final hydrolysis step because free threonine is fragile and hydrolysis typically gives threonine/allothreonine mixtures rather than a single target product.
suggestions: Explicitly note Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry as the operative catalyst system for rxn_003 and rxn_005.
State that rxn_008 yields threonine plus allothreonine racemate rather than threonine alone.
Include low-temperature or rapid-use conditions to address threonine instability.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong alternative route supported by Patel et al., where glyceraldehyde serves as a key branch point linking sugar and amino acid chemistry. The direct Strecker entry from glyceraldehyde to threonine aminonitrile is plausible and literature-supported. As with P1, product stereochemistry and threonine instability are underrepresented.
suggestions: Clarify whether glyceraldehyde formation proceeds under the same cyanosulfidic copper photoredox conditions throughout.
Represent threonine/allothreonine co-production explicitly.
Add mention of borate or selective sequestration chemistry if mixture control is desired.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is plausible because spark discharge chemistry is known to produce threonine-related precursors, and acetaldehyde is a reasonable reservoir product. However, the network overstates specificity in assigning a single spark step to HCN, NH3, acetaldehyde, and glycine simultaneously, and it does not clearly justify how those discharge products are transferred into the cyanosulfidic photoredox regime without destructive side chemistry.
suggestions: Treat spark discharge as a feedstock-generating reservoir rather than a clean stoichiometric reaction.
Separate atmospheric production and aqueous collection/concentration into distinct steps.
Document H2S-enhanced discharge conditions more explicitly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a chemically sensible hybrid pathway, but it is less direct and depends on combining two distinct source regimes: spark-discharge precursor generation and surface cyanosulfidic homologation. That combination is not impossible, yet the compatibility of reservoirs, concentrations, and redox states is not fully demonstrated.
suggestions: Add a concentration/transport step between atmospheric synthesis and surface pool chemistry.
Differentiate spark-derived glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde inputs from HCN photoredox-generated ones.
Address side-product complexity from discharge residues.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Bucherer-Bergs branch is a good inclusion and aligns with the literature discussion of hydantoins as thermodynamic sinks. The main weakness is that direct experimental validation for threonine from lactaldehyde hydantoin is less explicit than for alanine from pyruvate analogs, so some extrapolation is involved. Final hydantoin hydrolysis to free threonine remains plausible but condition-sensitive.
suggestions: Frame rxn_009 as literature-motivated extrapolation from demonstrated hydantoin chemistry rather than fully established for threonine.
Specify hydrolysis severity and note possible decomposition during prolonged aqueous treatment.
Mention meteoritic hydantoin detection as indirect support.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization to DAMN/polymeric reservoirs is well supported, but direct assignment of hydrolysis products to threonine as a discrete output is too strong. Literature supports amino acid mixtures containing threonine in some hydrolysates, not selective or robust threonine synthesis. The pathway is therefore weak as a target-directed route.
suggestions: Represent rxn_014 as producing a mixed amino acid pool rather than threonine specifically.
Add separation or selection chemistry if this branch is meant to contribute materially to threonine accumulation.
Reduce confidence claims for target specificity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest pathway. While the glycine-acetaldehyde relationship is biochemically meaningful, a purely prebiotic aldol condensation to threonine has not been experimentally demonstrated under convincing abiotic conditions and is thermodynamically unfavorable. It should be treated as highly speculative rather than a serious production route.
suggestions: Downgrade this branch to a conceptual side-route.
If retained, include a catalyst or activation scenario and clearly label it speculative.
Do not count this among core target-producing pathways without experimental support.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route captures an important literature idea—reductive amination of alpha-keto acids—but overextends the evidence. Pyruvate formation under hydrothermal iron-sulfur conditions is plausible, yet generation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from pyruvate plus acetaldehyde plus CO2 is not a well-established prebiotic step, and the direct reductive amination to threonine is only analogically supported. The route is best viewed as speculative.
suggestions: Explicitly mark both reactions as speculative extrapolations from alpha-keto acid reductive amination literature.
Add a reducing agent/catalyst system for rxn_016 such as Fe2+ or Ni/H2 if this branch is retained.
Avoid presenting hydrothermal threonine formation as a major route given threonine thermal fragility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hybrid hydrothermal-to-surface route is coherent at a systems level: reduced carbon from vents feeds surface cyanosulfidic chemistry that then accesses threonine aminonitrile and threonine. The weakness is that the specific connection from hydrothermal formate/pyruvate production to the chosen surface aldehyde network is not explicitly mechanistic in the listed reactions, so the inter-environment handoff is more conceptual than demonstrated.
suggestions: Add a clearer transport/conversion step linking hydrothermal carbon fixation products to aldehyde pools.
Keep hydrothermal temperatures in the mild alkaline vent range to avoid amino acid destruction.
Specify mineral catalysts consistently for the hydrothermal reactions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway appropriately addresses the literature-identified instability bottleneck by routing threonine into immediate downstream utilization. Clay-mediated wet-dry peptide formation is plausible, though representing peptide formation as a single-molecule self-condensation reaction is simplified and would typically require two threonine molecules plus dehydration. As a systems-level pathway, however, it is strong.
suggestions: Stoichiometrically represent peptide formation using two threonine molecules and water loss.
Specify montmorillonite and wet-dry cycling conditions explicitly.
Emphasize that peptide capture may protect threonine from degradation.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong and chemically mixed. Its major strengths are dense connectivity, multiple convergent target-producing routes, and good use of literature-supported hubs, especially HCN, acetaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile. The cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde Strecker branches are excellent and closely match the deep research findings. The Bucherer-Bergs branch is also a reasonable literature-grounded addition. The network further does well in recognizing threonine instability and partially addressing it via rapid peptide capture. However, several reactions are overspecified or too target-selective relative to literature: HCN polymer hydrolysis to threonine, glycine plus acetaldehyde aldol condensation, and hydrothermal keto-acid conversion to threonine are all significantly more speculative than the core cyanosulfidic route. Environmental interconnection is conceptually present, but the hydrothermal-to-surface handoff is not fully realized mechanistically. Another notable issue is compliance with the original synthesis-network specification: hydrothermal and surface reactions were listed without explicit mineral agents, despite the requirement that these be specified. Literature alignment is good for the central routes but weaker for peripheral branches. Overall, this is a good but not fully robust network; the core target production is convincing, but the total network confidence is reduced by speculative side pathways and incomplete environmental/mineral detail.
